# From McNally to Snyder: The Narrowing of Federal Public Corruption Law {#sec-appendix-honest-services}

```{=latex}
\setcounter{page}{1}
\renewcommand{\thepage}{C-\arabic{page}}
```

## Introduction: The Statutory Framework

The federal government's primary tools for prosecuting public corruption were not designed for that purpose. The mail fraud statute, enacted in 1872, was created to protect the integrity of the postal system from schemes that used the mails to defraud victims of money or property. The wire fraud statute, added in 1952, extended the same prohibition to schemes using interstate wire communications. Both statutes were written broadly, prohibiting any "scheme or artifice to defraud" that employed the covered instrumentality.

The original jurisdictional logic was straightforward: the federal government had authority to regulate the mails and interstate communications, so it could prohibit fraudulent schemes that exploited those federal systems. The substantive offense was the fraud; the use of mails or wires provided the federal hook. Over time, prosecutors recognized that this jurisdictional hook could reach virtually any fraud that involved a letter, phone call, or later an email—including corruption by state and local officials, so long as some communication crossed state lines or used the postal service.

By the mid-twentieth century, federal prosecutors had developed these statutes into powerful weapons against public corruption at all levels of government. The theory was that citizens had a right to the "honest services" of their public officials, and that officials who took bribes, engaged in self-dealing, or otherwise betrayed the public trust were defrauding the citizenry of that intangible right. The mail and wire fraud statutes, courts held, could reach such conduct even when the defendant did not obtain money or property from the government directly.

This interpretation transformed the mail and wire fraud statutes from narrow protections of federal instrumentalities into a broad federal common law of fiduciary duty. For four decades, prosecutors used this "intangible rights" theory to pursue corrupt officials who might otherwise escape prosecution under state laws that were limited, unenforced, or subject to local political pressure. The expansion was not without critics, who warned that the doctrine lacked clear boundaries and raised federalism concerns. But federal courts across the country embraced the approach, and by the 1980s, honest services fraud had become the workhorse of federal public corruption enforcement.

## The Intangible Rights Doctrine (1940s–1987)

The intangible rights doctrine emerged gradually through federal appellate decisions beginning in the 1940s. The Fifth Circuit's 1941 decision in [*United States v. Shushan*](https://law.justia.com/cases/federal/appellate-courts/F2/117/110/1480190/){.cite} is often cited as the doctrine's foundation. That case involved corruption in Louisiana bond issuances, and the court held that depriving citizens of their right to have their government function honestly was cognizable under the mail fraud statute. Over the following decades, courts across the country built on *Shushan* and similar precedents, recognizing that the mail fraud statute's prohibition on "schemes to defraud" could encompass more than the deprivation of money or tangible property.

The doctrinal rationale was rooted in fiduciary duty. A public official who sold his office, or a corporate fiduciary who secretly diverted business opportunities, was engaged in a "scheme to defraud" even if the victim could not point to a specific dollar amount taken. The fraud lay in the breach of trust itself. When citizens entrusted officials with governmental power, they had a right to expect that power would be exercised honestly. Officials who violated that trust—by taking bribes, engaging in self-dealing, or concealing conflicts of interest—defrauded the public of the honest services it was owed.

Federal appellate courts articulated the theory in fiduciary terms. The fraud statutes, courts reasoned, reached beyond traditional property fraud to protect relationships of trust. Public officials owed a fiduciary duty to the citizens they served; betrayal of that duty through self-dealing, bribery, or undisclosed conflicts of interest constituted a scheme to defraud the public of its right to honest government.

By the mid-1980s, every federal circuit had adopted some version of the intangible rights doctrine. The Third Circuit had applied it to Philadelphia officials who took payments to steer city contracts. The Seventh Circuit had used it against Chicago aldermen involved in zoning corruption. The Second Circuit had prosecuted New York officials for taking payments in exchange for regulatory forbearance. The specific formulations varied: some courts emphasized the official's duty of loyalty, others focused on the deprivation of honest services, and still others grounded liability in the failure to disclose material information. But the core concept was consistent. A public official who abused his position for personal gain—whether through bribes, kickbacks, self-dealing, or undisclosed conflicts—could be prosecuted under the mail fraud statute if any aspect of the scheme involved use of the mails.

The doctrine's breadth was both its strength and its vulnerability. Prosecutors valued the flexibility to reach corruption that might not fit state bribery statutes, which often required proof of an explicit agreement to exchange official action for payment. Many real-world corruption schemes were more subtle: officials who steered business to friends and relatives, who made decisions affecting their own financial interests, or who accepted benefits with the understanding (never explicitly stated) that favorable treatment would follow. The intangible rights doctrine allowed federal prosecutors to reach this conduct even when state law did not.

Defense attorneys and some judges worried that the lack of clear boundaries created risks of arbitrary enforcement and encroachment on areas traditionally left to state regulation. What distinguished criminal corruption from aggressive lobbying, or from the routine exchange of favors that characterized much of political life? Critics argued that the doctrine gave prosecutors standardless discretion to decide what conduct was criminal. The lack of statutory definition left courts and prosecutors to draw lines that Congress had never drawn.

## McNally v. United States (1987): The First Narrowing

The Supreme Court addressed these concerns in [*McNally v. United States*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/483/350/){.cite}, decided in 1987. The case involved a scheme by Kentucky state officials to steer the state's workers' compensation insurance business to a particular agency in exchange for kickbacks. Charles McNally served as chairman of the Kentucky Democratic Party; James Gray was Kentucky's Secretary of Public Protection and Regulation and later Secretary of the Governor's Cabinet. The two men arranged for the state's workers' compensation insurance to be placed with a specific agency, Seton Investments, which was secretly owned by Howard Hunt, an associate who shared the resulting commissions with McNally and Gray.

The officials did not directly take money from the state treasury; rather, they ensured that certain insurance commissions went to favored parties, who then shared the proceeds. The scheme was classic public corruption: public officials using their positions to enrich themselves and their associates. But the direct victim of the fraud—the entity deprived of money—was not the Commonwealth of Kentucky. The state paid the same insurance premiums it would have paid regardless of which agency placed the coverage. The money that McNally and Gray received came from commissions that would have gone to other insurance agents in a competitive marketplace.

The federal government charged McNally and Gray with mail fraud, arguing that their scheme deprived Kentucky citizens of their intangible right to the honest and faithful services of their public officials. The Sixth Circuit affirmed the convictions, following the then-universal circuit consensus that honest services fraud was a valid application of the mail fraud statute.

The Supreme Court reversed by a vote of 7–2. Writing for the majority, Justice White held that the mail fraud statute was "limited in scope to the protection of property rights." The Court examined the statute's legislative history, which traced back to 1872, and found no indication that Congress intended to create a "federal common law of crimes" governing the ethical conduct of public officials. The "scheme to defraud" language required proof that the defendant sought to obtain money or property; it could not be stretched to cover the deprivation of the citizenry's abstract right to honest government.

"Rather than construe the statute in a manner that leaves its outer boundaries ambiguous and involves the Federal Government in setting standards of disclosure and good government for local and state officials," Justice White wrote, "we read § 1341 as limited in scope to the protection of property rights. If Congress desires to go further, it must speak more clearly than it has."

Justice Stevens dissented, joined by Justice O'Connor. Stevens argued that the majority had gutted the statute by ignoring decades of consistent judicial interpretation. The mail fraud statute had been used to prosecute public corruption since at least 1941, and Congress had never indicated disapproval. To the contrary, Congress had repeatedly revised the mail fraud statute without disturbing the settled judicial interpretation. By the time of *McNally*, the intangible rights doctrine was settled law. The majority's narrow reading, Stevens argued, rested on a theory of statutory interpretation that ignored the statute's actual operation and effect.

The immediate impact was substantial. Pending prosecutions were dismissed. Convictions were vacated. In the Southern District of New York alone, the U.S. Attorney's office had to dismiss or rethink dozens of cases. Federal prosecutors who had relied on the intangible rights theory to pursue state and local corruption found their primary tool eliminated. The *McNally* decision did not prohibit all federal prosecution of public corruption—bribery cases involving direct payment for official acts could still proceed under 18 U.S.C. § 201 (for federal officials) or the Hobbs Act (for extortion)—but it significantly narrowed federal reach over the gray areas of self-dealing, conflicts of interest, and undisclosed benefits that characterized much real-world corruption.

## Congressional Response: Section 1346 (1988)

Congress responded to *McNally* within eighteen months. The legislative process was expedited: members of Congress expressed concern that corrupt officials were escaping justice due to a judicial decision that disregarded Congress's evident intent to reach public corruption. In November 1988, as part of the omnibus Anti-Drug Abuse Act, Congress added a single sentence to the federal criminal code: "For the purposes of this chapter, the term 'scheme or artifice to defraud' includes a scheme or artifice to deprive another of the intangible right of honest services." Codified as [18 U.S.C. § 1346](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/1346){.cite}, this provision restored the intangible rights doctrine that *McNally* had eliminated.

The legislative history was sparse—a fact that would become significant in later litigation. Congress did not define "honest services," did not specify whose honest services were covered, and did not indicate what conduct would constitute a deprivation of honest services. The floor statements accompanying the provision were brief. The Senate Judiciary Committee report stated simply that the amendment was intended to "overturn the McNally decision" and restore the law to what it had been before that ruling. The statute effectively incorporated by reference the pre-*McNally* case law, leaving courts to determine the doctrine's precise contours.

This legislative approach was both efficient and problematic. It was efficient because it allowed Congress to act quickly, restoring federal prosecutorial authority without engaging in the difficult work of defining the boundaries of honest services fraud. The pre-*McNally* case law had developed over four decades; attempting to codify it precisely would have required substantial legislative drafting. It was problematic because the pre-*McNally* case law was itself inconsistent across circuits, and because it left the fundamental vagueness concerns identified by critics unaddressed.

For the next two decades, federal prosecutors resumed using the honest services theory to pursue public corruption cases. Courts generally applied the pre-*McNally* standards, which had never been fully harmonized across circuits. Some courts required proof of a violation of state law as a predicate for honest services fraud; others held that federal fiduciary standards controlled. Some courts required proof that the defendant gained personal benefit; others focused on the breach of duty regardless of personal gain. Some courts required proof of an intent to deceive the public; others held that the violation of fiduciary duty was itself sufficient. The doctrinal variations persisted, but prosecutions continued.

The theory expanded beyond public officials to reach private-sector fraud as well. Corporate executives who breached their fiduciary duties to shareholders, employees who took secret kickbacks from vendors, union officials who accepted payments from employers, and others who violated positions of trust could be prosecuted for depriving their principals of honest services. The doctrine's application to private conduct generated its own controversies—what exactly was the source of the duty that private employees owed, and to whom was it owed?—but the public corruption cases remained the statute's core application.

Between 1988 and 2010, federal prosecutors used Section 1346 to pursue thousands of public corruption cases. The statute became indispensable to the Department of Justice's Public Integrity Section, which coordinates federal prosecution of public officials. High-profile cases included prosecutions of governors, members of Congress, state legislators, judges, and local officials across the country. The honest services theory was particularly valuable in cases involving the gray areas of corruption—officials who did not take outright bribes but who used their positions for personal enrichment in ways that betrayed the public trust.

## [Skilling v. United States](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/561/358/){.cite} (2010): The Second Narrowing

The Supreme Court returned to honest services fraud in 2010, in a case arising from the collapse of Enron Corporation. Enron's bankruptcy in December 2001 was, at the time, the largest corporate bankruptcy in American history, resulting in losses of billions of dollars for investors and employees. Federal prosecutors charged numerous Enron executives with various crimes, including honest services fraud. Jeffrey Skilling, Enron's former CEO, was convicted of conspiracy to commit honest services fraud, securities fraud, and other charges. He received an initial sentence of over 24 years in prison (later reduced to 14 years on resentencing).

Skilling's challenge to his honest services fraud conviction presented the Supreme Court with a question it had not directly addressed since *McNally*: was Section 1346 unconstitutionally vague? Skilling argued that the statute's failure to define "honest services" left prosecutors and courts with standardless discretion to decide what conduct was criminal. The statute criminalized breaches of fiduciary duty, but it did not specify what duties were covered, how serious the breach must be, or what mental state was required. In effect, Skilling argued, the statute invited prosecutors to enforce their own views about ethical conduct rather than standards established by Congress.

The government defended the statute by arguing that twenty years of post-*McNally* case law had given Section 1346 sufficiently determinate content. Courts had developed standards for honest services fraud that, while not perfect, provided adequate notice of what conduct was prohibited. The fact that the statute did not contain detailed definitions did not render it unconstitutionally vague, the government contended, because many federal criminal statutes employed broad terms that courts had defined through case-by-case adjudication.

The Court agreed that the statute presented serious vagueness concerns, but declined to invalidate it entirely. Writing for the majority, Justice Ginsburg employed a "limiting construction" to save the statute from unconstitutionality. Section 1346, the Court held, criminalizes only "bribery and kickback schemes"—the "core" of the pre-*McNally* case law. These were the paradigmatic cases that had given rise to the honest services doctrine: officials who accepted payments in exchange for favorable official action. Other applications of the honest services theory, including prosecution for undisclosed self-dealing and conflicts of interest, fell outside the statute's reach.

"It has always been as combating bribes and kickbacks," the Court explained, "that the honest-services doctrine has retained vitality." The pre-*McNally* cases consistently and uniformly identified bribes and kickbacks as the core of honest services fraud. While some courts had extended the doctrine beyond this core to reach undisclosed conflicts of interest and self-dealing, these extensions were neither as consistent nor as well-established. By limiting Section 1346 to the bribery-and-kickback core, the Court could preserve the statute's clearly constitutional applications while eliminating the vague outer reaches.

The decision eliminated a substantial category of conduct that had been prosecuted as honest services fraud. Self-dealing—using one's position to benefit oneself without a third-party payment—was no longer covered. Undisclosed conflicts of interest, absent a bribe or kickback, could not form the basis for prosecution. An official who steered contracts to companies in which he held secret investments, or who made decisions affecting his own financial interests without disclosure, was not committing honest services fraud unless someone paid him to do it.

Justice Scalia concurred in the judgment but wrote separately to argue that the limiting construction did not cure the statute's vagueness. The terms "bribery" and "kickback" were themselves undefined in Section 1346, and their meaning varied across jurisdictions. Some states defined bribery narrowly to require an explicit quid pro quo; others defined it more broadly. Some courts required proof of a specific agreement to exchange payment for action; others inferred agreement from circumstances. "Even if the Court chose the clearest of the pre-McNally applications of the statute to prosecute," Scalia wrote, "that would not render that application constitutional." The majority's attempt to save the statute, Scalia argued, merely replaced one vague standard with another. He would have held the statute unconstitutional in its entirety.

Justice Ginsburg's majority opinion acknowledged the force of Scalia's critique but concluded that the bribery-and-kickback core had sufficiently clear contours. Bribery and kickbacks had been illegal for centuries; their elements were well-established in federal and state law. The term "bribe" was not ambiguous in ordinary usage, and federal courts had decades of precedent defining what constituted a kickback. By limiting Section 1346 to this core, the Court preserved Congress's clear intent to prohibit the most paradigmatic forms of public corruption while eliminating the outer reaches of the doctrine that had generated vagueness concerns.

## Failed Legislative Responses (2010–2012)

Congress's immediate response to *Skilling* suggested legislative concern about the decision's impact. Within months, members introduced bills to restore the honest services prosecutions that *Skilling* had eliminated.

Senator Patrick Leahy, then Chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee, introduced the Honest Services Restoration Act ([S. 3854](https://www.govtrack.us/congress/bills/111/s3854/text){.cite}) in September 2010, just three months after the *Skilling* decision. The bill would have amended Section 1346 to explicitly cover "undisclosed self-dealing by public officials"—the category of conduct that *Skilling* had removed from the statute. Under the proposal, a federal, state, or local official who "knowingly" failed to disclose material information about "a personal financial interest" and who took official action "that he or she knew or should have known would directly benefit that financial interest" would be guilty of honest services fraud. The bill sought to address the vagueness concerns identified in *Skilling* by providing specific definitions and a clear scienter requirement. The bill was reintroduced in the House in the following Congress as H.R. 1468, sponsored by Representative Bruce Braley.

The proposal generated opposition from defense attorneys and civil liberties organizations. The National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers (NACDL) submitted testimony calling the bill "constitutionally suspect" and "dangerously vague." NACDL argued that the terms "personal financial interest" and "material information" remained undefined and could encompass an enormous range of conduct. A local official who voted on a zoning matter affecting property values in his neighborhood might be criminally liable; a state legislator who voted on tax policy that affected her own tax burden might be covered. Critics argued that the legislation would criminalize routine political activity without providing clear standards for enforcement. Prosecutors already had ample tools to pursue corruption, opponents contended; extending honest services fraud to self-dealing would simply invite arbitrary enforcement.

A broader reform effort, the Clean Up Government Act, sought to address public corruption more comprehensively. Sponsored by Representatives Mike Quigley (D-IL) and James Sensenbrenner (R-WI) in a bipartisan effort, the bill (H.R. 1793, later [H.R. 2572](https://www.congress.gov/bill/112th-congress/house-bill/2572){.cite}) would have extended statutes of limitations for public corruption offenses from five years to six years, increased criminal penalties, expanded federal wiretap authority to include additional corruption offenses, and restored the self-dealing theory eliminated by *Skilling*. The bipartisan sponsorship reflected a shared concern across the political spectrum about the erosion of federal anti-corruption enforcement.

The House Judiciary Committee reported the bill favorably in December 2011 by a vote of 19–8. When the Senate considered anti-corruption provisions as part of the STOCK Act (prohibiting insider trading by members of Congress), the Senate version included language based on the Clean Up Government Act. This would have achieved the restoration advocates sought through must-pass legislation.

But the provisions did not survive conference. The House stripped the Clean Up Government Act provisions from the final bill before passage. The stated concern was that the provisions were beyond the scope of the STOCK Act, which was narrowly focused on congressional insider trading. Critics of the provisions argued that they represented an attempt to achieve by legislative rider what could not be achieved through the normal legislative process.

Neither the Honest Services Restoration Act nor the Clean Up Government Act became law. The legislation was never reintroduced in subsequent Congresses. The *Skilling* limitation remained in place, and federal prosecutors adjusted their charging practices accordingly. The Justice Department's Public Integrity Section continued to bring honest services cases, but only where the evidence supported a bribery or kickback theory. The gray areas of corruption (undisclosed self-dealing, conflicts of interest, favoritism without explicit payment) remained largely beyond federal reach.

## McDonnell v. United States (2016): The Third Narrowing

Six years after *Skilling*, the Supreme Court further narrowed federal corruption law in [*McDonnell v. United States*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/579/15-474/){.cite}. The case involved Robert McDonnell, who served as Governor of Virginia from 2010 to 2014, and his wife Maureen. The facts were not in significant dispute. A Virginia businessman named Jonnie Williams, the CEO of a company called Star Scientific, had provided the McDonnells with over $175,000 in gifts, loans, and benefits over a two-year period.

The items Williams provided to the McDonnells included: a Rolex watch worth approximately $6,500; Oscar de la Renta and Louis Vuitton designer goods totaling over $20,000 during a single New York shopping trip; approximately $15,000 for wedding catering for the McDonnells' daughter; a check for $15,000 to cover a wedding gift from the McDonnells to their daughter; $10,000 to cover costs of the wedding reception; clothing and accessories worth over $10,000 for Mrs. McDonnell, including dresses for the Republican National Convention; two loans totaling $120,000 to help with the McDonnells' real estate holdings and other debts; and a golf outing at an exclusive club. Williams also paid for vacations and allowed the McDonnells to use his Ferrari.

In exchange, Governor McDonnell arranged meetings between Williams and state officials to discuss research studies that might validate Star Scientific's dietary supplement product; hosted events at the Governor's mansion to promote the company, including a launch event for the product attended by state officials; and contacted other government officials, including the Secretary of Health and Human Resources, to encourage them to consider studies of the product at state universities. Williams's goal was to have Virginia's state universities conduct clinical studies that would validate his company's product, which would increase its value and marketability.

The jury convicted McDonnell of honest services fraud and Hobbs Act extortion. The Fourth Circuit affirmed. The Supreme Court unanimously reversed.

The question was the meaning of "official act" under [18 U.S.C. § 201](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/201){.cite}, the federal bribery statute, which defines the quid pro quo required for corruption offenses. The statute defines "official act" as "any decision or action on any question, matter, cause, suit, proceeding or controversy, which may at any time be pending, or which may by law be brought before any public official, in such official's official capacity, or in such official's place of trust or profit."

The Court held that arranging meetings, hosting events, and making phone calls on Williams's behalf did not constitute "official acts" under this definition. To qualify as an official act, conduct must involve a formal exercise of governmental power—a "decision or action" on a "question, matter, cause, suit, proceeding or controversy" that is pending or may be pending before the government. Setting up a meeting, even a meeting with other government officials, is not itself a decision on a pending matter. It might lead to such a decision, but the meeting itself is not the exercise of governmental power. Hosting an event is not a formal governmental action. Contacting other officials to recommend they take action is not the same as taking that action oneself; it is an attempt to influence, not an exercise of authority.

Chief Justice Roberts, writing for the Court, acknowledged that the government's evidence "may have shown that petitioner's conduct was 'distasteful' and 'tawdry.'" The evidence revealed an "unseemly" relationship in which a businessman showered gifts on a governor and his wife while seeking favorable consideration from state government. Reasonable people might disagree, Roberts noted, about whether McDonnell's conduct was "an appropriate way for the Governor to behave."

But federal criminal law, the Court emphasized, reaches only conduct that falls within the statutory definition of bribery. That definition requires proof that the official agreed to perform or performed an "official act" in exchange for payment. If the conduct at issue does not qualify as an official act, no federal bribery offense has occurred, regardless of how corrupt the arrangement may appear. The Court expressed concern that an expansive interpretation would "chill federal officials' interactions with the people they serve" and would leave officials uncertain about what conduct might subject them to prosecution.

The Justice Department declined to retry McDonnell. On September 8, 2016, less than three months after the Supreme Court's decision, the government filed a motion to dismiss all charges. McDonnell, who had served as Governor of Virginia and had been considered a potential vice-presidential candidate, never faced further prosecution for the conduct at issue. He had served no prison time, his conviction having been stayed pending appeal.

## Convictions Overturned After McDonnell (2016–2018)

*McDonnell* had immediate ripple effects on other public corruption prosecutions. Within two years, several high-profile convictions were overturned or significantly reduced based on the decision's narrow definition of "official act."

Sheldon Silver had served as Speaker of the New York State Assembly for over two decades, making him one of the three most powerful officials in New York state government (along with the Governor and Senate Majority Leader). In November 2015, a federal jury convicted Silver of honest services fraud and extortion. The government's theory was that Silver had received approximately $4 million in payments—characterized as legal referral fees from two law firms—in exchange for taking official actions benefiting parties connected to those firms. One law firm referred asbestos cases to Silver, and in exchange, Silver allegedly directed state funds to a doctor who was referring mesothelioma patients to that firm. The other firm represented real estate developers who benefited from rent regulation laws that Silver championed. Silver received referral fees for clients he never met and work he never performed; the fees were, prosecutors argued, bribes disguised as legal compensation.

Silver was sentenced to twelve years in prison—one of the longest sentences ever imposed in a New York corruption case. But in July 2017, the Second Circuit vacated his conviction, holding that the jury instructions failed to require proof of an official act as *McDonnell* defined that term. The instructions had allowed the jury to convict based on Silver's general advocacy and legislative activities, which were not "official acts" under the narrowed definition. Silver was retried in 2018. The government secured a second conviction, but on a narrower set of charges that more clearly satisfied the *McDonnell* standard. Silver received a reduced sentence of seven years, later further reduced to six and a half years. He reported to prison in August 2020 and died in January 2022 while incarcerated.

Dean Skelos had served as Majority Leader of the New York State Senate, the chamber's top Republican position. In December 2015, he was convicted alongside his son Adam for pressuring companies with business before the state to provide Adam with payments and employment. The scheme involved at least three companies: one paid Adam $200,000 for minimal work; another hired him as a consultant; a third paid him for environmental consulting work that he barely performed. The elder Skelos allegedly used his official position to benefit these companies, creating the expectation that they would continue to compensate his son.

The Second Circuit vacated those convictions in September 2017, citing the same *McDonnell* problem. The jury had been instructed that an official act could include any action taken in an official capacity, which was inconsistent with *McDonnell*'s requirement of a formal decision on a pending government matter. Skelos was retried in 2018, convicted again, and sentenced to four years and three months—significantly less than his original five-year sentence. He was released in April 2020 after serving approximately eighteen months, in part due to COVID-19 concerns.

Perhaps most dramatic was the case of William Jefferson, the Louisiana Congressman found with $90,000 in cash, wrapped in aluminum foil and frozen in food containers, in his freezer. FBI agents discovered the cash during a 2005 raid of Jefferson's home; the serial numbers matched bills that had been provided to a cooperating witness in a bribery investigation. Jefferson was convicted in 2009 of bribery, racketeering, and money laundering, among other charges. He received a sentence of thirteen years—the longest sentence ever given to a member of Congress for bribery-related offenses.

After *McDonnell*, Jefferson sought post-conviction relief. In October 2017, a federal judge held that seven of his ten remaining counts should be thrown out because the charged conduct did not involve official acts under the narrowed definition. The government had alleged that Jefferson had taken various actions to assist business ventures in Africa, but many of those actions—making introductions, attending meetings, writing letters of support—were not formal exercises of governmental power. Jefferson was resentenced and released from prison in December 2017 after serving approximately five and a half years of his original thirteen-year sentence.

The pattern across these cases was consistent. Conduct that juries had found to be corrupt—and that, in ordinary language, clearly constituted corruption—did not fit the legal definitions that the Supreme Court had established. The officials had received substantial benefits from people seeking government action. They had taken steps to assist those benefactors. But the steps they took were not "official acts" as defined in *McDonnell*, and therefore no federal crime had been committed under the statutes charged.

## Kelly v. United States (2020): The Fourth Narrowing

The Supreme Court continued to narrow federal corruption law in [*Kelly v. United States*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/590/18-1059/){.cite}, arising from the scandal known as "Bridgegate." The facts were these: In September 2013, the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey closed two of three local access lanes from Fort Lee, New Jersey, to the George Washington Bridge. The closures lasted four days and caused massive traffic gridlock in Fort Lee. Schoolchildren were late to school. Emergency responders had difficulty reaching calls. Commuters sat in traffic for hours. The official explanation given at the time was that the lane closures were part of a "traffic study."

The explanation was false. The real purpose of the lane closures, as evidence later established, was political retaliation. The mayor of Fort Lee, Mark Sokolich, had declined to endorse Governor Chris Christie's reelection campaign. In response, Christie aides decided to punish the mayor by creating traffic chaos in his city. Bridget Anne Kelly, Christie's deputy chief of staff, sent an email to David Wildstein, a Christie appointee at the Port Authority, that read: "Time for some traffic problems in Fort Lee." Wildstein implemented the plan, and Bill Baroni, another Christie appointee, provided the cover story about a traffic study.

Kelly and Baroni were convicted of wire fraud and fraud on a federally funded program (the Port Authority received federal funds). The government's theory was that they had obtained the Port Authority's property—the bridge lanes themselves—through fraud, and that they had obtained Port Authority employees' labor under false pretenses by lying about the purpose of the lane closures.

The Supreme Court unanimously reversed. Justice Kagan, writing for the Court, acknowledged that the evidence "no doubt shows wrongdoing—deception, corruption, abuse of power." Kelly and Baroni "used deception to reduce Fort Lee's access lanes to the George Washington Bridge—and thereby jeopardized the safety of the town's residents." Their actions caused "the destruction of the normal lanes—and thereby, for a period of time, the normal patterns of access to the bridge." The conduct was, in every ordinary sense, corrupt.

But federal fraud statutes, the Court held, require a scheme to obtain money or property. The defendants did not seek to obtain the bridge lanes for themselves; they sought to use them for a regulatory purpose, albeit a corrupt one. "The realignment of the toll lanes was an exercise of regulatory power," the Court explained—"something this Court has already held fails to meet the federal fraud statutes' property requirement." The employees' labor was not property that the defendants obtained through fraud; the employees would have done the same work regardless of the defendants' motives. The lie about the traffic study did not deprive the Port Authority of its employees' labor; it merely concealed the true purpose of a governmental decision.

"Not every corrupt act by state or local officials is a federal crime," Justice Kagan wrote. "Because the scheme here did not aim to obtain money or property, Baroni and Kelly could not have violated the federal-program fraud or wire fraud laws." The political retaliation at the heart of Bridgegate was reprehensible, but it was not fraud. Federal fraud statutes protect property interests, not the general interest in honest government. When officials misuse their regulatory power for corrupt purposes, but do not seek to obtain money or property for themselves, the federal fraud statutes do not apply.

The decision attracted attention for what it did not do as well as what it did. Justice Kagan's opinion did not question the facts established at trial, did not suggest that the defendants' conduct was appropriate, and did not criticize the prosecutors for bringing the case. The Court simply held that the conduct, however improper, did not fit the elements of the charged offenses. If the people of New Jersey or their elected representatives wished to punish such conduct, state law was the appropriate vehicle.

## The Buffalo Billion Cases (2023): The Fifth Narrowing

The Supreme Court decided two related cases in May 2023 that further limited federal prosecutors' tools against public corruption. Both arose from Governor Andrew Cuomo's "Buffalo Billion" initiative, a major infrastructure investment program that promised to revitalize the economy of upstate New York through state-funded construction projects.

The Buffalo Billion program, announced in 2012, committed $1 billion in state funds to economic development in the Buffalo region. The state's flagship project was a $750 million contract to build a factory that would manufacture solar panels, eventually known as the SolarCity facility. The contract-award process was managed through a state-affiliated nonprofit called Fort Schuyler Management Corporation. Federal prosecutors alleged that the bidding process was rigged.

In [*Ciminelli v. United States*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/598/21-1170/){.cite}, Louis Ciminelli, a Buffalo construction executive and significant political donor, was convicted of wire fraud for his role in the rigged bidding. The evidence showed that Ciminelli's company had received inside information about the contract requirements and had been assured by a lobbyist connected to the Cuomo administration that it would win the contract regardless of the actual bidding. The "competition" was a sham; the outcome was predetermined.

The prosecution proceeded on the "right to control" theory. Under this theory, developed in the Second Circuit over several decades, fraud could be established by showing that the defendant deprived the victim of "potentially valuable economic information." The state of New York, in this view, had a right to control its contracting decisions based on complete and accurate information. By rigging the process and depriving the state of the information that competition would have been futile, Ciminelli had committed fraud even if the state received the facility it contracted for at the agreed price.

The Supreme Court unanimously rejected the right-to-control theory. Justice Thomas, writing for the Court, held that the federal fraud statutes reach only schemes to deprive victims of traditional property interests—money, tangible property, or intangible property rights recognized at common law. "The right to valuable economic information needed to make discretionary economic decisions" is not such a property interest. Depriving someone of valuable information may be tortious, and it may be criminalized by state law, but it does not constitute federal wire fraud. The victim must actually be deprived of money or property, not merely of information that might have led to different decisions.

The decision eliminated a prosecution tool that federal prosecutors had used for decades. The right-to-control theory had allowed prosecutors to reach corruption in contracting that did not fit traditional theft or bribery categories. An official or private party who rigged a bidding process—ensuring that a favored bidder won even if the government received what it contracted for—had committed fraud under this theory. After *Ciminelli*, such conduct may not be federally prosecutable unless it involves traditional bribery, kickbacks, or actual theft of government funds.

In [*Percoco v. United States*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/598/21-1158/){.cite}, decided the same day, the Court addressed honest services fraud in a different context. Joseph Percoco had been Governor Cuomo's Executive Deputy Secretary, a powerful position that made him one of the most influential figures in New York state government. In 2014, Percoco temporarily left government employment to manage Cuomo's reelection campaign. During this period, while technically a private citizen, Percoco accepted $35,000 from a real estate company to influence state agency decisions affecting the company's projects. He contacted state officials and advocated for the company's interests, using his relationship with the governor and his knowledge of state government to advance the company's goals.

The question was whether Percoco, as a private citizen, could be convicted of honest services fraud. The government argued that Percoco retained a duty of honest services to the public even while off the government payroll, because of his "special relationship" with the government and his continuing ability to "dominate and control" government business. The jury was instructed that it could convict if it found that Percoco occupied "a position of trust and confidence" with the state and exercised "de facto control" over government decisions.

The Supreme Court unanimously held that the jury instructions were too vague to satisfy due process. The "special relationship" and "dominance and control" standards provided no clear guidance about when a private citizen owes a fiduciary duty to the public. Many people have special relationships with government officials. Many lobbyists and advisors exercise influence over government decisions. The instructions did not distinguish Percoco's situation from countless others in which private citizens seek to influence government action.

Justice Gorsuch, concurring, wrote that the honest services statute continued to suffer from the vagueness problems that had plagued it from the beginning. "After today's decision," he observed, "we may now know a little bit more about when a duty of honest services does not arise, but we still have no idea when it does." The limiting construction in *Skilling* had not solved the statute's fundamental problems; it had merely relocated them.

## [Snyder v. United States](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/603/23-108/){.cite} (2024): The Sixth Narrowing

The Supreme Court's most recent public corruption decision came in June 2024. James Snyder had served as mayor of Portage, Indiana. During his tenure, the city awarded contracts worth approximately $1.1 million to a local trucking company. After the contracts were awarded, the trucking company paid Snyder $13,000. Federal prosecutors charged Snyder with accepting an illegal gratuity under [18 U.S.C. § 666](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/666){.cite}, which prohibits theft, bribery, and other corrupt conduct affecting programs that receive federal funds.

The question was whether Section 666 prohibits only bribes—payments made in advance to influence official action—or also gratuities—payments made after the fact to reward official action. The statute's text prohibited "corruptly" soliciting or accepting "anything of value" "intending to be influenced or rewarded" in connection with official action. The government argued that "rewarded" indicated that after-the-fact payments were covered; Snyder argued that the statute reached only bribes.

The Supreme Court ruled 6–3 for Snyder. Justice Kavanaugh, writing for the majority, held that Section 666 covers bribes to state and local officials, but not gratuities. The majority reasoned that the statute's structure, history, and purpose all pointed toward this interpretation. Congress had separately prohibited gratuities to federal officials, using different language and imposing lesser penalties. If Congress had wanted to criminalize gratuities to state and local officials, it would have done so explicitly.

The majority also invoked federalism concerns. Criminalizing gratuities to state and local officials, Justice Kavanaugh wrote, would raise "significant federalism concerns" because states have their own laws governing gifts to public officials. The federal government should not displace state regulation of state officials' ethics without a clearer indication from Congress.

Justice Jackson dissented, joined by Justices Sotomayor and Kagan. The dissent characterized the majority's reading as "absurd" and "atextual," arguing that the plain language of Section 666 covered rewards as well as bribes. The practical effect, Jackson wrote, was to immunize after-the-fact payments to state and local officials from federal prosecution—"a get-out-of-jail-free card" for corruption thinly disguised as gratuities.

## Legislative Response to Snyder (2024–Present)

Within weeks of the *Snyder* decision, members of Congress introduced legislation to close the gratuities loophole. The No Gratuities for Governing Act, sponsored by Representatives Jeff Jackson and Dan Crenshaw in a bipartisan effort, would amend Section 666 to explicitly cover gratuities as well as bribes. The bill was introduced in August 2024 and reintroduced in the following Congress.

As of early 2026, the legislation remains pending. No floor votes have been held in either chamber. The pattern that emerged after *Skilling*—congressional concern followed by legislative inaction—appears to be repeating.

## The Current State of Federal Public Corruption Law

The cumulative effect of the Supreme Court's decisions from *McNally* through *Snyder* has been to substantially narrow the federal government's ability to prosecute public corruption at the state and local level.

Federal prosecutors retain effective authority to pursue certain categories of corruption. Traditional bribery cases—where there is evidence of an explicit quid pro quo agreement between the official and the payer, involving a formal exercise of governmental power—remain viable. Kickback schemes, where officials receive payments as a percentage of contracts they steer, can be prosecuted under honest services fraud as interpreted in *Skilling*. Theft of government property, where officials embezzle public funds or convert government resources to personal use, falls outside the honest services framework and remains fully prosecutable. Tax fraud charges are available when officials fail to report corrupt payments as income. And RICO prosecutions, which require proof of a pattern of racketeering activity, can aggregate corruption offenses that might individually be insufficient for prosecution.

What federal prosecutors cannot effectively charge, after the sequence of Supreme Court decisions, includes a substantial range of conduct that ordinary citizens would recognize as corrupt. Undisclosed self-dealing by public officials (steering contracts to companies in which the official has a hidden interest, for example) falls outside the *Skilling* bribery-and-kickback limitation. Conflicts of interest without third-party payment, where officials make decisions affecting their own financial interests without disclosure, are not covered. Gratuities to state and local officials (payments made after favorable official action, as "thank you" gifts rather than advance bribes) are not prohibited by Section 666 after *Snyder*. "Access" corruption, involving the sale of meetings, introductions, and influence, does not involve "official acts" as *McDonnell* defines that term. And political retaliation that does not involve obtaining money or property, as in *Kelly*, falls outside the fraud statutes entirely.

The enforcement data reflects this narrowing. According to data from the Transactional Records Access Clearinghouse at Syracuse University, federal prosecution of state and local officials for public corruption declined by 49 percent between 2003 and 2022. The Justice Department's Public Integrity Section has prosecuted fewer cases, and the cases it does prosecute tend to involve more straightforward bribery allegations where the government can meet the heightened standards established by the Supreme Court.

International comparisons also suggest increasing concern about U.S. corruption. Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index, which rates countries based on expert assessments of public-sector corruption, scored the United States at [65 out of 100](https://www.transparency.org/en/cpi/2024/index/usa){.cite} in 2024 (with 100 being least corrupt), its lowest score in over a decade. The United States now ranks behind most Western European democracies and several Asian nations on this measure. While the index measures perceptions rather than objective corruption levels, the declining trend suggests that experts believe corruption is either increasing or, at minimum, not being adequately addressed.

The shift to state enforcement, which the Supreme Court has repeatedly invoked as the appropriate response to narrowed federal jurisdiction, faces significant obstacles. Many states lack official misconduct statutes that could fill the federal gap. States without such statutes rely on common law crimes, specific bribery statutes that may have their own limitations, or no applicable criminal provisions at all. State prosecutors may face political pressure not to pursue state officials, particularly in one-party jurisdictions where the prosecutor and the target belong to the same political coalition. State attorney general offices and district attorney offices may lack the resources and expertise that federal prosecutors bring to complex corruption cases. The U.S. Attorney's offices that historically prosecuted state and local corruption have specialized units, grand jury resources, and investigative tools—including FBI support—that most state prosecutors cannot match.

The enforcement gap that federal honest services fraud was designed to address—the concern that state-level corruption would go unpunished because state enforcement mechanisms were compromised or inadequate—remains.

## The Federalism Dimension

Throughout these decisions, the Supreme Court has emphasized federalism as a reason to interpret federal corruption statutes narrowly. The concern is that overly broad federal statutes would allow federal prosecutors to intrude into areas traditionally regulated by states—specifically, the ethics and conduct of state and local officials.

The federalism rationale appears in various forms across the key decisions. In *McNally*, the Court noted that Congress had not clearly intended to federalize all breaches of fiduciary duty by state officials. Reading the mail fraud statute to reach intangible rights, the Court observed, would effectively create "a federal common law of crimes" that "involves the Federal Government in setting standards of disclosure and good government for local and state officials." If Congress wanted to go that far, it needed to "speak more clearly."

In *McDonnell*, the Court warned against an interpretation that would "intrude into states' jurisdiction" over the conduct of their own officials. A broad reading of "official act," the Court reasoned, would mean that "the Federal Government could use the criminal law to enforce ethical standards that state and local governments have chosen not to adopt." Officials would be subject to federal prosecution for conduct that their own states had decided not to criminalize. The First Amendment concerns the Court identified—the risk of chilling communications between officials and constituents—were in part concerns about federal regulation of state political processes.

In *Kelly*, the Court stated explicitly that federal fraud statutes "leave much public corruption to the States (or their electorates) to rectify." The sentence has been quoted frequently in subsequent litigation and commentary. It reflects a vision of dual sovereignty in which states bear primary responsibility for regulating the conduct of their officials, with federal intervention reserved for conduct that clearly falls within federal statutory prohibitions.

In *Snyder*, the majority cited "significant federalism concerns" as a reason to exclude gratuities from Section 666. States regulate gifts to state officials through their own ethics laws, which vary considerably. Some states ban gratuities; others permit them within limits; others impose disclosure requirements without criminal penalties. Interpreting Section 666 to criminalize gratuities would impose a uniform federal standard that would override these varying state approaches. The Court concluded that Congress had not clearly indicated an intent to displace state regulation in this area.

The counterargument, advanced in dissents and academic commentary, is that corruption itself undermines federalism. When state and local officials are corrupt, state systems of accountability fail. Corrupt officials may control the very institutions—attorney general offices, state ethics commissions, legislative oversight committees—that are supposed to hold them accountable. Local prosecutors may be politically allied with or personally connected to the officials they would need to investigate. State criminal laws may be inadequate, state enforcement resources may be limited, and state political cultures may tolerate corruption that would be prosecuted elsewhere.

Federal prosecution, on this view, serves as a backstop when state enforcement is compromised by the very corruption it should address. The historical expansion of federal public corruption law—from the intangible rights doctrine through Section 1346—responded to a real problem: state-level corruption that states could not or would not address. The Framers, some scholars argue, understood that corruption at all levels of government threatened the republic, and the Constitution reflects an "anti-corruption legacy" that supports congressional power to address misconduct by state officials even when states themselves fail to act.

The tension remains unresolved. Federal jurisdiction over state and local corruption has contracted significantly since 1987. The contraction has not been accompanied by a corresponding expansion of state enforcement capacity. Whether state enforcement will grow to fill the gap, whether federal legislation will restore some of the lost prosecutorial tools, or whether the enforcement gap will persist indefinitely, remains to be seen. What is clear is that the Supreme Court has repeatedly concluded that federalism concerns favor narrow interpretation of federal corruption statutes, leaving states with the primary responsibility for addressing misconduct by their officials.

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Timeline: Key Developments in Federal Public Corruption Law

| Year | Development |
|------|-------------|
| 1872 | Mail fraud statute enacted |
| 1952 | Wire fraud statute enacted |
| 1940s–1980s | Intangible rights doctrine develops in circuit courts |
| 1987 | *McNally v. United States*: fraud limited to money or property |
| 1988 | Congress enacts Section 1346, restoring honest services fraud |
| 2010 | *Skilling v. United States*: honest services limited to bribes and kickbacks |
| 2010–2012 | Honest Services Restoration Act, Clean Up Government Act introduced; neither passes |
| 2016 | *McDonnell v. United States*: "official act" narrowly defined |
| 2017–2018 | Silver, Skelos, Jefferson convictions overturned or reduced |
| 2020 | *Kelly v. United States*: fraud requires scheme to obtain property |
| 2023 | *Ciminelli v. United States*: right-to-control theory rejected |
| 2023 | *Percoco v. United States*: private-citizen honest services theory limited |
| 2024 | *Snyder v. United States*: gratuities to state/local officials excluded |
| 2024–present | No Gratuities for Governing Act introduced; pending |
